perturbation.original_text: 8. RETURN OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION 8.1.1. Upon Discloserȇs request, Recipient shall promptly, and in any case within five (5) days from such request, return all documentation and other materials containing any Confidential Information of Discloser without retaining any copies thereof. Alternatively, at Discloserȇs option and request, Recipient shall destroy and/or erase all such materials and documentation and shall provide a written certification that all such materials and documentation have been destroyed and/or erased.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. RETURN OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION 8.1.1. Upon Discloserȇs request, Recipient may return documentation and other materials containing any Confidential Information of Discloser. At Discloser’s option, Recipient may destroy such materials.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires the recipient to return or destroy confidential information within 5 days of request. The modified text removes the mandatory nature of the return or destruction and removes the deadline, weakening the protection of confidential information. This creates a potential legal issue under Danish law, specifically concerning trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 23(1) of the Danish Act on Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_citation: Section 23(1) of the Danish Act on Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens the obligation to return or destroy confidential information, potentially contradicting Section 23(1) of the Danish Act on Trade Secrets, which implies a stronger obligation to protect and return trade secrets to prevent unlawful acquisition, use, or disclosure. The original text aligns better with protecting trade secrets by enforcing a prompt return or destruction, whereas the modified text makes it discretionary, thus potentially violating the spirit of the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Denmark - Protecting Intellectual Property Skip to main content Official Website of the International Trade Administration Here’s how you know Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( A locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Denmark Country Commercial Guide Learn about the market conditions, opportunities, regulations, and business conditions in denmark, prepared by at U.S. Embassies worldwide by Commerce Department, State Department and other U.S. agencies’ professionals Denmark Country Commercial Guide Doing Business in Denmark Market Overview Market Challenges Market Opportunities Market Entry Strategy Other Areas in the Kingdom of Denmark Leading Sectors for US Exports & Investments Renewable Energy Products Digital Services and Technologies Smart Cities Healthcare and Lifescience Products and Services Travel and Tourism Services Agricultural Sector Customs, Regulations & Standards Trade Barriers Import Tariffs Import Requirements and Documentation Value Added Tax (VAT) Labeling/Marking Requirements U.S. Export Controls Temporary Entry Prohibited & Restricted Imports Customs Regulations Trade Agreements Standards for Trade Licensing Requirements for Professional Services Selling US Products & Services Distribution and Sales Channels eCommerce Selling Factors and Techniques Trade Financing Protecting Intellectual Property Selling to Public Sector Business Travel Business Travel Digital Economy Digital Economy Investment Climate Statement Investment Climate Statement Select Country Guide Protecting Intellectual Property Last published date: 2024-01-20 In general, Denmark offers adequate protection for intellectual property rights. According to the Danish Copyright Act, anyone who produces a literary or artistic work has copyright on that work. The copyright holder has the sole right to control the work by producing copies of it and making it available to the public. Copyright on a work e.g., music, literature, film, photo, etc.) lasts for 70 years after the copyright holder’s death. Other groups of people with rights also enjoy protection under the Danish Copyright Act: performing artists, record and film producers, radio and TV broadcasters, and photographers, as well as catalogues and databases. For related rights, the copyright lasts for 50 years after it is set (i.e., the recording or broadcast is made). For catalogues and databases, however, the protection only lasts until 15 years have passed from the end of the year in which the work was first made available to the public. The copyright originates when the work is created, and the legal protection comes into force without any formal requirements having to be met. There is therefore no public register in which copyright is registered. Key link and more information: https://europa.eu/youreurope/business/running-business/intellectual-property/index_en.htm See Section VI for a list of the international conventions and treaties concerning intellectual property to which Denmark adheres. In any foreign market, companies should consider several general principles for effective management of their intellectual property. For background on these principles please link to the following article on Protecting Intellectual Property and Stopfakes.gov , or contact ITA’s Office of Intellectual Property Rights Director, Stevan Mitchell, Stevan.Mitchell@trade.gov . For additional information on the protection and enforcement of intellectual property rights, visit the U.S. Investment Climate Statement website .
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the recipient to return or destroy confidential information within a specific timeframe (5 days), aligning with a strong obligation to protect trade secrets. The changed text weakens this obligation by making the return or destruction discretionary and removing the deadline. This contradicts the intent of protecting intellectual property, as the snippet from the International Trade Administration states that 'Denmark offers adequate protection for intellectual property rights' and implies a responsibility to control and protect literary and artistic works. While the snippet focuses on copyright, the broader principle of protecting intellectual property is undermined by the modified text's weakening of obligations regarding confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: 5.3. Ownership – no rights intended 5.3.1. All Confidential Information shall remain the property of Discloser or its relevant Representative, as applicable. 5.3.2. The disclosure of Confidential Information to Recipient or its Representatives shall not give Recipient or its Representatives any licence or other rights in relation to that Confidential Information beyond the rights contained in this Agreement. 5.3.3. Recipient shall not be entitled to file for patents or other statutory protection in any country based on any Confidential Information received hereunder. The disclosure of Confidential Information does not constitute any rights of prior use for Recipient
perturbation.changed_text: 5.3. Ownership – no rights intended 5.3.1. All Confidential Information shall remain the property of Discloser or its relevant Representative, as applicable. 5.3.2. The disclosure of Confidential Information to Recipient or its Representatives shall not give Recipient or its Representatives any licence or other rights in relation to that Confidential Information beyond the rights contained in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the sentence "Recipient shall not be entitled to file for patents or other statutory protection in any country based on any Confidential Information received hereunder", we create a legal loophole where the recipient could attempt to patent the disclosed information, which may violate Danish intellectual property laws protecting trade secrets and inventions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 1 of the Danish Patents Act
perturbation.law_citation: Section 1 of the Danish Patents Act
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing the explicit prohibition on patenting confidential information creates a contradiction with the intent of Section 1 of the Danish Patents Act. While the Act itself doesn't explicitly forbid patenting information received under NDA, it implicitly protects against the misappropriation of trade secrets by outlining the requirements for patentability, which include novelty and inventive step. If the recipient patents confidential information without adding an inventive step, this can be construed as a violation of the disclosing party's rights.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: WIPO - Search International and National Patent Collections Processing Please wait... Please select the picture containing a blue eye # -
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly prohibited the recipient from patenting confidential information. By removing this clause, the modified text creates a legal loophole where the recipient could attempt to patent disclosed information. The Perturbation Explanation highlights this loophole and the potential violation of intellectual property laws. The Law Explanation supports the argument that the absence of a prohibition on patenting may allow for misappropriation of trade secrets, undermining the disclosing party's rights. The lack of a scraped law snippet does not negate the potential violation outlined in the Perturbation and Law explanations.

perturbation.original_text: 5.1. Recipient shall: 5.1.1. hold Confidential Information in confidence and protect it by using at least the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, as Recipient uses to safeguard its own confidential or proprietary information of a like nature from unauthorized use, disclosure or dissemination; 5.1.2. use Confidential Information only for the Project; 5.1.3. not copy or reproduce (or permit to be copied or reproduced) any Confidential Information, or directly or indirectly disclose or distribute any of it to any person other than those of its Representatives who are strictly required to perform Recipientȇs work in relation to the Project.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1. Recipient shall: 5.1.1. hold Confidential Information in confidence; 5.1.2. use Confidential Information only for the Project; 5.1.3. not copy or reproduce any Confidential Information, or directly or indirectly disclose or distribute any of it to any person other than those of its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'and protect it by using at least the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, as Recipient uses to safeguard its own confidential or proprietary information of a like nature from unauthorized use, disclosure or dissemination,' the contract no longer specifies a standard of care. This creates a loophole because the recipient is not explicitly obligated to actively protect the confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 4 of the Danish Act on Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_citation: Section 4 of the Danish Act on Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing the required standard of care weakens the protection of trade secrets and confidential information. Section 4 of the Danish Act on Trade Secrets addresses the unlawful acquisition, use, and disclosure of trade secrets. By removing the obligation to protect the information with a reasonable degree of care, the agreement becomes less compliant with the spirit of the law, as it doesn't explicitly enforce active protection against potential misappropriation of trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the explicit obligation for the recipient to protect confidential information with a defined standard of care ('at least the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care'). The Perturbation Explanation clarifies that this omission creates a loophole, weakening the protection of the confidential information. The Law Explanation further supports this, stating that removing the standard of care makes the agreement less compliant with the law, as it does not explicitly enforce active protection against potential misappropriation of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that Denmark offers adequate protection for intellectual property rights. By omitting the 'reasonable degree of care', the contract fails to provide adequate protection, contradicting the general intent of intellectual property protection laws in Denmark.

perturbation.original_text: 7.3. Continuing Obligations 7.3.1. The obligations of confidentiality, non-disclosure and non-use set forth in this Confidentiality Agreement shall survive the termination or expiration of this Confidentiality Agreement (i) with respect to any Confidential Information that constitutes a trade secret under applicable law, for so long as such item shall continue to constitute a trade secret under applicable law, and (ii) with respect to any Confidential Information that does not constitute a trade secret under applicable law, for a period of 5 (five) years from and after the date of disclosure of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.3. Continuing Obligations 7.3.1. The obligations of confidentiality, non-disclosure and non-use set forth in this Confidentiality Agreement shall survive the termination or expiration of this Confidentiality Agreement for a period of 1 (one) year from and after the date of disclosure of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause differentiates between trade secrets (protected indefinitely) and other confidential information (protected for 5 years). The modified clause removes this distinction and sets a fixed 1-year protection period for all confidential information. This significantly weakens the protection of trade secrets, potentially violating Danish law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 5 of the Danish Act on Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_citation: Section 5 of the Danish Act on Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: By limiting the protection of all confidential information, including trade secrets, to a single year, the modified clause contradicts Section 5 of the Danish Act on Trade Secrets, which aims to protect trade secrets from unlawful acquisition, use, or disclosure for as long as they remain trade secrets. By not providing indefinite protection for actual trade secrets, the agreement potentially enables their misappropriation after the one-year period, undermining the law's objective.

perturbation.justification: The original contract ensures indefinite protection for trade secrets, while the modified version limits protection to one year for all confidential information, including trade secrets. The LLM's law explanation states that this contradicts the Danish Act on Trade Secrets, which aims to protect trade secrets for as long as they remain trade secrets. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't directly address trade secrets law, it broadly affirms that Denmark offers 'adequate protection for intellectual property rights,' suggesting that indefinite protection of trade secrets is a legally supported principle that is undermined by the contract change.
